content.text: GenAI systems accumulate technical debt faster than conventional software because the system has more moving parts: the model, the prompt, the retrieval layer, the eval suite, the deployment infrastructure, and the data pipeline. Each is a site for debt accumulation. A technical debt register is the mechanism that makes this debt visible, prioritized, and scheduled for repayment — before it compounds into an incident or a quality regression.

attrs.alt: GenAI technical debt taxonomy showing four categories: Prompt Debt, Eval Debt, RAG/Data Debt, and Infra/Ops Debt, with interest rate concept and severity classification

content.text: Why GenAI Technical Debt Is Different

Conventional software technical debt is primarily about code quality: missing tests, tangled abstractions, deprecated dependencies. GenAI systems add four new debt categories that conventional debt registers do not capture:
Undocumented decisions, hardcoded values, unexplained few-shot examples
Quality drifts as the model updates; nobody knows what the prompt is doing or why
Missing coverage, stale labels, no regression detection
Regressions ship silently; team discovers quality problems from users, not alerts
Stale index, unvalidated chunk strategy, missing PII scrub
Retrieval quality decays as source data ages; privacy risk accumulates invisibly
Pinned deprecated runtimes, uncapped token limits, missing runbooks
Incidents are harder to respond to; blast radius grows with each skipped upgrade

The interest rate concept is particularly important for GenAI debt. A missing eval case does not cause an error today — it causes a regression that ships undetected in three months. The debt has accumulated invisibly, and the payment is a production quality incident.

The technical debt register is a structured list of known debt items. It is not a backlog of work to do (that is the sprint backlog) — it is a  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'visibility and tracking artifact', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  that links debt items to their origin, their cost of delay, and their planned remediation quarter.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Literal

DebtCategory = Literal[
    "prompt_debt",
    "eval_debt",
    "rag_data_debt",
    "infra_ops_debt",
    "process_debt",      # missing checklists, unclear ownership
    "security_debt",     # PII exposure risk, missing auth, unrotated keys
]

InterestRate = Literal[
    "low",        # quality/efficiency degrades slowly; low urgency
    "medium",     # silent regression risk; monitor closely
    "high",       # active quality or reliability impact
    "critical",   # safety or security risk; pay immediately
]

Severity = Literal["S1", "S2", "S3", "S4"]

@dataclass
class DebtItem:
    id: str
    category: DebtCategory
    title: str                          # verb phrase: "Missing...", "Undocumented..."
    description: str                    # what exists now and why it is debt
    interest_rate: InterestRate         # cost per week of not addressing this
    interest_description: str           # concrete: what gets worse if deferred
    severity: Severity                  # S1=now, S2=this quarter, S3=next quarter, S4=icebox
    affected_component: str             # inference | rag | eval | prompt | infra | data
    effort_days: float                  # engineering-days to resolve
    owner: str
    target_quarter: str | None          # e.g. "2026-Q3" (None = icebox)
    origin: str                         # "launch-retro" | "incident" | "audit" | "review"
    jira_ticket: str | None = None
    blocked_by: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)   # IDs of other debt items

    @property
    def monthly_interest_cost(self) -> str:
        rate_map = {
            "low": "Negligible near-term cost; watch quarterly",
            "medium": "Growing blind spot; one regression ships per month on average",
            "high": "Active quality drag; detectable in weekly metrics",
            "critical": "Immediate risk; do not defer past current sprint",
        }
        return rate_map[self.interest_rate]

from datetime import date

register = [
    DebtItem(
        id="TD-038",
        category="prompt_debt",
        title="Hardcoded date in document QA system prompt",
        description=(
            "The system prompt contains 'As of January 2026, ...' as a hardcoded string. "
            "This was added during the beta sprint to pass an eval case. "
            "As of 2026-07, the date is already stale."
        ),
        interest_rate="high",
        interest_description=(
            "Responses claim information is current as of January 2026. "
            "Quality degrades each month as the date drifts further from reality."
        ),
        severity="S1",
        affected_component="prompt",
        effort_days=0.5,
        owner="fe-engineer@company.com",
        target_quarter="2026-Q3",
        origin="launch-retro",
    ),
    DebtItem(
        id="TD-039",
        category="eval_debt",
        title="No eval coverage for multi-page PDF table extraction",
        description=(
            "The eval suite has 0 test cases covering table extraction from "
            "PDFs where tables span page boundaries. "
            "This gap was identified in the 30-day post-launch review when "
            "an enterprise customer escalated a bug in this scenario."
        ),
        interest_rate="medium",
        interest_description=(
            "Any prompt or chunking change that degrades table extraction will "
            "ship without detection. The bug rate in this scenario is unknown."
        ),
        severity="S2",
        affected_component="eval",
        effort_days=3.0,
        owner="qe-lead@company.com",
        target_quarter="2026-Q3",
        origin="incident",
        jira_ticket="GENAI-1847",
    ),
    DebtItem(
        id="TD-040",
        category="rag_data_debt",
        title="Vector index has no TTL — stale content is never evicted",
        description=(
            "The Pinecone index is populated at document upload time. "
            "There is no mechanism to evict vectors for deleted or updated source documents. "
            "The index currently contains vectors for 340 documents that have been "
            "removed from the source system."
        ),
        interest_rate="high",
        interest_description=(
            "Responses can cite deleted documents or superseded content. "
            "The stale vector count grows by approximately 20/week."
        ),
        severity="S2",
        affected_component="rag",
        effort_days=4.0,
        owner="ml-engineer@company.com",
        target_quarter="2026-Q3",
        origin="audit",
    ),
    DebtItem(
        id="TD-041",
        category="security_debt",
        title="Embedding API key is hardcoded in deployment config",
        description=(
            "The OpenAI embedding API key is stored as a literal string in "
            "k8s/production/configmap.yaml. It should be in a Kubernetes secret."
        ),
        interest_rate="critical",
        interest_description=(
            "Any repository read access (GitHub Actions logs, accidental push, "
            "CI artifact) exposes the key. No rotation schedule exists."
        ),
        severity="S1",
        affected_component="infra",
        effort_days=1.0,
        owner="platform-eng@company.com",
        target_quarter="2026-Q3",
        origin="audit",
    ),
]

Severity maps debt items to a pay schedule. The labels S1–S4 are intentionally different from the P0–P3 incident severity scale to avoid confusion between operational incidents (which need immediate response) and debt items (which have a planned remediation window).
Blocking graduation, active safety/security risk, cost leak
Hardcoded API key, uncapped token limit, 0 eval cases in CI
Silent regression risk, incident amplifier, compliance gap
Stale RAG index, missing runbook for known failure mode
Developer efficiency, code quality, coverage gaps
Undocumented few-shot examples, missing eval for edge cases
Low interest rate, no active risk
Deprecated utility function, alternative chunking strategy to evaluate
The debt register is reviewed quarterly, not weekly. Debt review is a strategic activity — it is about deciding what to repay over the next quarter, not firefighting.

## Quarterly Technical Debt Review — GenAI Platform
**Attendees:** Engineering lead, PM, SRE lead, Security (if any S1 security items)
**Output:** Approved S1/S2 items with owners and sprint assignments

### 1. New items since last review (20 min)
- Walk through items added to register since last quarter
- Confirm category, interest rate, severity for each
- Identify any that should be immediate (S1 not yet in a sprint)

### 2. S1 item status (15 min)
- Are all S1 items in the current sprint or already closed?
- Any S1 items deferred without a resolution plan?
  → Escalate to Director if yes

### 3. Interest rate changes (15 min)
- Have any S3/S4 items increased in urgency due to new incidents or model changes?
- Re-classify if so; may need sprint slot this quarter

### 4. Capacity allocation (20 min)
- How many engineering-days are allocated to debt repayment this quarter?
- Standard: 15–20% of engineering capacity
- Prioritize: all S1 → highest-interest S2 → S2 by effort (quick wins)
- Assign owners and target sprints

### 5. Icebox review (10 min)
- Any S4 items older than 6 months?
  → Verify interest rate has not risen; archive if truly obsolete

### 6. Register health check (10 min)
- Are items described specifically enough to execute without re-investigation?
- Missing owners? Missing effort estimates?
- Any items that should be split into smaller pieces?

Teams that allocate no formal capacity to debt repayment experience compounding debt. The standard allocation is 15–20% of engineering capacity per quarter, but the right number depends on the current debt severity distribution:
10–15% — system is healthy, maintain standard cadence
> 3 S1 items or any safety-category S1
30–40% until S1 items are cleared — escalate to leadership
Rapid S3→S2 migration trend (interest rate rising)
Increase to 25% — prevent backlog from compounding
New debt items enter the register from four sources. Each source should have a defined pathway to ensure debt is captured, not just mentioned in passing:

— the post-launch retrospective action item categories (prompt hacks, eval gaps, manual deploy steps) map directly to debt categories. The PM converts retrospective items into debt register entries within 1 week of the retrospective

— when an incident's root cause identifies a structural gap (missing runbook, missing circuit breaker, stale index), the corresponding debt item is added to the register by the SRE lead before the postmortem document is closed

— a structured review of prompt files, eval suites, RAG configurations, and deployment configs looking for debt items not yet in the register. Typically run by the engineering lead 2 weeks before the quarterly review
— when a reviewer identifies debt during a standard PR review, they add a debt register item in addition to leaving a PR comment. The PR does not need to be blocked, but the debt is captured

def audit_prompt_files_for_debt(prompt_directory: str) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Scans prompt files for common debt patterns and returns candidate register items.
    Run this as part of the quarterly debt audit.
    """
    import os, re

    patterns = [
        (r"As of \d{4}", "hardcoded_date",
         "Date reference will become stale. Inject current date dynamically."),
        (r"# TODO|# FIXME|# HACK", "inline_todo",
         "Inline TODO/FIXME in prompt file — should be a registered debt item."),
        (r"temperature\s*=\s*0\.[0-9]+", "unvalidated_temperature",
         "Temperature value may not be documented as intentional. Verify and document."),
        (r"(example|few.?shot)[^\n]{0,60}\n(\s*[-*]|Q:|User:)", "magic_few_shot",
         "Few-shot examples with no documentation of why these examples were chosen."),
    ]

    candidates = []
    for fname in os.listdir(prompt_directory):
        if not fname.endswith((".txt", ".md", ".yaml", ".json")):
            continue
        path = os.path.join(prompt_directory, fname)
        with open(path) as f:
            content = f.read()
        for pattern, debt_type, description in patterns:
            if re.search(pattern, content, re.IGNORECASE):
                candidates.append({
                    "file": fname,
                    "debt_type": debt_type,
                    "description": description,
                    "suggested_category": "prompt_debt",
                    "suggested_severity": "S3",
                })
    return candidates

The debt register is not a static document — it must be connected to sprint planning. The engineering lead brings the register to each sprint planning session and proposes a slate of debt items to include.

def propose_sprint_debt_items(
    register: list[DebtItem],
    available_capacity_days: float,
    sprint_id: str,
) -> list[DebtItem]:
    """
    Selects debt items to propose for a sprint based on severity and effort.
    Prioritizes S1 items first, then S2 by effort (quick wins), then S2 by interest rate.
    """
    # Filter: only items without a sprint assigned yet
    unscheduled = [item for item in register if item.target_quarter and not item.jira_ticket]

    # Sort: S1 first, then by interest rate severity, then by effort (quick wins)
    priority = {"S1": 0, "S2": 1, "S3": 2, "S4": 3}
    interest_priority = {"critical": 0, "high": 1, "medium": 2, "low": 3}

    sorted_items = sorted(
        unscheduled,
        key=lambda x: (priority[x.severity], interest_priority[x.interest_rate], x.effort_days)
    )

    # Greedily fill capacity
    selected, total = [], 0.0
    for item in sorted_items:
        if total + item.effort_days <= available_capacity_days:
            selected.append(item)
            total += item.effort_days
        if total >= available_capacity_days:
            break

    return selected

# Example: 4 days of debt capacity in sprint 2026-Q3-S01
sprint_items = propose_sprint_debt_items(register, available_capacity_days=4.0, sprint_id="2026-Q3-S01")
for item in sprint_items:
    print(f"  [{item.severity}] {item.id}: {item.title} ({item.effort_days}d)")
The register itself must be monitored. A register that is written once and never updated provides false confidence — the team believes debt is tracked when it is not.
Engineering lead estimates before next review
Review interest rate — may need to escalate to S1
Increase debt allocation or reduce feature work
A technical debt register for GenAI teams makes the four categories of GenAI-specific debt visible and manageable:
— prompt, eval, RAG/data, and infra/ops debt each accumulate differently and require different remediation approaches
— quantifies the cost of delay; a missing eval case is low interest today and medium interest when the next regression ships silently
— distinct from operational incident severity; maps debt to a pay schedule (now / this quarter / next quarter / icebox)
— retrospectives, postmortems, quarterly audits, and code review each have a defined pathway into the register
— formal sprint capacity for debt repayment prevents compounding; increase to 30–40% when S1 items accumulate
— strategic debt review happens quarterly, not weekly; sprint planning pulls from the already-prioritized register
